section_title: Title and extent of operation of the Code
chunk_text: This Act shall be called the Indian Penal Code, and shall extend to the whole of India except the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 1 historical extent applicability excluding Jammu and Kashmir pre 2019 territorial jurisdiction criminal law

section_title: Title and extent of operation of the Code
chunk_text: This Act shall be called the Indian Penal Code, and shall extend to the whole of India.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 1 current extent applicability whole of India post 2019 territorial jurisdiction criminal law

section_title: Extension of Code to extra-territorial offences
chunk_text: The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by— (1) any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 4 Extension of Code to extra-territorial offences. Indian citizens committing crimes abroad.

section_title: Extension of Code to extra-territorial offences
chunk_text: Explanation.— In this section— (a) the word “offence” includes every act committed outside India which, if committed in India, would be punishable under this Code;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 4 Explanation extra-territorial offence definition. Acts committed outside India.

chunk_text: Whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person, is said to do that thing “dishonestly”.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 24 Definition of Dishonestly. Intention of wrongful gain or wrongful loss.

chunk_text: Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder, if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 300 Firstly murder definition intention to cause death. Culpable homicide vs murder.

chunk_text: Culpable homicide is not murder if the offender, whilst deprived of the power of self-control by grave and sudden provocation, causes the death of the person who gave the provocation or causes the death of any other person by mistake or accident.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 300 Exception 1 grave and sudden provocation. When murder is reduced to culpable homicide.

chunk_text: (a) A shoots Z with the intention of killing him. Z dies in consequence. A commits murder.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 300 Illustration (a) shooting with intention to kill. Example of murder.

chunk_text: Whoever voluntarily has carnal intercourse against the order of nature with any man, woman or animal, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

references.target: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India

embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 377 Unnatural offences carnal intercourse against order of nature. LGBT rights Navtej Singh Johar.

section_title: Definitions in the Code to be understood subject to exceptions
chunk_text: Throughout this Code every definition of an offence, every penal provision, and every illustration of every such definition or penal provision, shall be understood subject to the exceptions contained in the Chapter entitled “General Exceptions”, though those exceptions are not repeated in such definition, penal provision, or illustration. [cite: 13]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 6 Definitions subject to General Exceptions. Interpretation of penal provisions and offences. [cite: 13]

section_title: Definitions in the Code to be understood subject to exceptions
chunk_text: (a) The sections, in this Code, which contain definitions of offences, do not express that a child under seven years of age cannot commit such offences, but the definitions are to be understood subject to the general exception which provides that nothing shall be an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age. [cite: 14]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 6 Illustration child under seven years of age. Exception to criminal liability for minors. [cite: 14]

chunk_text: (Third) — Every Judge including any person empowered by law to discharge, whether by himself or as a member of any body of persons, any adjudicatory functions; [cite: 37]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 21 Definition of Public Servant - Judges and adjudicatory officers. [cite: 37]

chunk_text: Explanation 2.— Wherever the words “public servant” occur, they shall be understood of every person who is in actual possession of the situation of a public servant, whatever legal defect there may be in his right to hold that situation. [cite: 49]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 21 Explanation 2 Public servant de facto status regardless of legal defects. [cite: 49]

section_title: Acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention
chunk_text: When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone. [cite: 79]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 34 Joint liability criminal act furtherance of common intention. Group crimes and shared responsibility. [cite: 79]

chunk_text: The punishments to which offenders are liable under the provisions of this Code are— (First) — Death; (Secondly) — Imprisonment for life; (Fourthly) — Imprisonment, which is of two descriptions, namely:— (1) Rigorous, that is, with hard labour; (2) Simple; (Fifthly) — Forfeiture of property; (Sixthly) — Fine. [cite: 119, 120, 121]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 53 Types of punishments under Indian Penal Code: Death sentence, life imprisonment, rigorous imprisonment, simple imprisonment, forfeiture, and fine. [cite: 119, 120, 121]

section_title: Act done by a person bound, or by mistake of fact believing himself bound, by law
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it. [cite: 165]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 76 General Exception mistake of fact vs mistake of law. Bound by law defense in good faith. [cite: 165]

section_title: Act done by a person bound, or by mistake of fact believing himself bound, by law
chunk_text: (a) A, a soldier, fires on a mob by the order of his superior officer, in conformity with the commands of the law. A has committed no offence. [cite: 166, 167]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 76 Illustration soldier firing on mob under legal superior orders. Defense of being bound by law. [cite: 166, 167]

section_title: Act of Judge when acting judicially
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 77 General Exception judicial immunity for Judges acting in good faith within legal power[cite: 168].

section_title: Act done pursuant to the judgment or order of Court
chunk_text: Nothing which is done in pursuance of, or which is warranted by the judgment or order of, a Court of Justice; if done whilst such judgment or order remains in force, is an offence, notwithstanding the Court may have had no jurisdiction to pass such judgment or order, provided the person doing the act in good faith believes that the Court had such jurisdiction.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 78 General Exception acts done following court orders or judgments in good faith[cite: 169, 170].

section_title: Act done by a person justified, or by mistake of fact believing himself justified, by law
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 79 General Exception justified by law mistake of fact defense in good faith[cite: 171].

section_title: Act done by a person justified, or by mistake of fact believing himself justified, by law
chunk_text: A sees Z commit what appears to A to be a murder. A, in the exercise, to the best of his judgment exerted in good faith, of the power which the law gives to all persons of apprehending murderers in the fact, seizes Z, in order to bring Z before the proper authorities. A has committed no offence, though it may turn out that Z was acting in self-defence.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 79 Illustration A seizing Z for apparent murder in good faith. Mistake of fact defense[cite: 172, 173, 174].

section_title: Accident in doing a lawful act
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 80 General Exception accident in lawful act without criminal intent and with proper care[cite: 175].

section_title: Act likely to cause harm, but done without criminal intent, and to prevent other harm
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 81 General Exception necessity doctrine act done to prevent greater harm without criminal intent[cite: 178].

section_title: Act of a child under seven years of age
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 82 General Exception absolute immunity for child under 7 years doli incapax[cite: 184].

section_title: Act of a person of unsound mind
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 84 General Exception legal insanity defense act of person of unsound mind incapable of understanding act[cite: 186].

section_title: Act of a person incapable of judgment by reason of intoxication caused against his will
chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, is, by reason of intoxication, incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong, or contrary to law; provided that the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 85 General Exception involuntary intoxication defense. Incapable of knowing nature of act due to intoxication without consent.

section_title: Offence requiring a particular intent or knowledge committed by one who is intoxicated
chunk_text: In cases where an act done is not an offence unless done with a particular knowledge or intent, a person who does the act in a state of intoxication shall be liable to be dealt with as if he had the same knowledge as he would have had if he had not been intoxicated, unless the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 86 Liability for offences requiring specific intent or knowledge during intoxication. Presumption of knowledge for voluntary intoxication.

chunk_text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 96 Right of Private Defence foundation. Acts done in self-defense are not offences.

section_title: Right of private defence of the body and of property
chunk_text: Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend— (First) — His own body, and the body of any other person, against any offence affecting the human body;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 97 First Right of private defence of the human body. Defending self or others against bodily offences.

section_title: Right of private defence of the body and of property
chunk_text: (Secondly) — The property, whether movable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 97 Secondly Right of private defence of property. Defending movable or immovable property against theft, robbery, mischief, or trespass.

section_title: Acts against which there is no right of private defence
chunk_text: The right of private defence in no case extends to the inflicting of more harm than it is necessary to inflict for the purpose of defence.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 99 Restriction on right of private defence. Proportionality of harm inflicted during defense. Necessary harm only.

section_title: When the right of private defence of the body extends to causing death
chunk_text: The right of private defence of the body extends, under the restrictions mentioned in the last preceding section, to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the assailant, if the offence which occasions the exercise of the right be of any of the descriptions hereinafter enumerated, namely:— (First) — Such an assault as may reasonably cause the apprehension that death will otherwise be the consequence of such assault;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 100 First Private defence extending to death. Apprehension of death from assault justifies causing death.

section_title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
chunk_text: If the offence be not of any of the descriptions enumerated in the last preceding section, the right of private defence of the body does not extend to the voluntary causing of death to the assailant, but does extend, under the restrictions mentioned in section 99, to the voluntary causing to the assailant of any harm other than death.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 101 Private defence of body not extending to death. Causing harm other than death when assault is not capital. [cite: 258]

section_title: When the right of private defence of property extends to causing death
chunk_text: The right of private defence of property extends, under the restrictions mentioned in section 99, to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the wrong-doer, if the offence... be an offence of any of the descriptions hereinafter enumerated, namely:— (First) — Robbery;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 103 First Private defence of property extending to death for Robbery. [cite: 261]

section_title: Commencement and continuance of the right of private defence of property
chunk_text: The right of private defence of property against robbery continues as long as the offender causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint or as long as the fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant personal restraint continues.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 105 Duration of private defence against robbery. Apprehension of instant death or hurt. [cite: 267]

chunk_text: A person abets the doing of a thing, who— (First) — Instigates any person to do that thing;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 107 First Definition of Abetment through instigation. [cite: 274]

chunk_text: Explanation 1.— A person who, by wilful misrepresentation, or by wilful concealment of a material fact which he is bound to disclose, voluntarily causes or procures, or attempts to cause or procure, a thing to be done, is said to instigate the doing of that thing.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 107 Explanation 1 Instigation by wilful misrepresentation or concealment. [cite: 277]

chunk_text: When two or more persons agree to do, or cause to be done,— (1) an illegal act, or (2) an act which is not illegal by illegal means, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy: Provided that no agreement except an agreement to commit an offence shall amount to a criminal conspiracy unless some act besides the agreement is done by one or more parties to such agreement in pursuance thereof.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 120A Definition of Criminal Conspiracy. Agreement to do illegal act or use illegal means. [cite: 368]

section_title: Waging, or attempting to wage war, or abetting waging of war, against the Government of India
chunk_text: Whoever, wages war against the Government of India, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 121 Waging war against Government of India. Death penalty or life imprisonment for insurrection and abetting war against the state.

chunk_text: Whoever, by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representation, or otherwise, brings or attempts to bring into hatred or contempt, or excites or attempts to excite disaffection towards, the Government established by law in India, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, to which fine may be added, or with imprisonment which may extend to three years, to which fine may be added, or with fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 124A Sedition definition and punishment. Hatred contempt or disaffection against the Government of India.

chunk_text: Explanation 2.— Comments expressing disapprobation of the measures of the Government with a view to obtain their alteration by lawful means, without exciting or attempting to excite hatred, contempt or disaffection, do not constitute an offence under this section.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 124A Explanation 2. Lawful criticism and disapprobation of government measures is not sedition.

chunk_text: An assembly of five or more persons is designated an “unlawful assembly”, if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is—
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 141 Unlawful assembly definition. Minimum five persons with a common object.

chunk_text: (Third) — To commit any mischief or criminal trespass, or other offence;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 141 Third common object of unlawful assembly: Committing mischief, criminal trespass, or other offences.

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
chunk_text: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 149 Constructive liability for members of an unlawful assembly. Liability for offences committed in prosecution of the common object.

chunk_text: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 299 Culpable homicide definition. Intent to cause death or knowledge of likelihood of death.

chunk_text: (a) A lays sticks and turf over a pit, with the intention of thereby causing death, or with the knowledge that death is likely to be thereby caused. Z believing the ground to be firm, treads on it, falls in and is killed. A has committed the offence of culpable homicide.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 299 Illustration (a) trap for causing death. Example of culpable homicide through intentional act.

chunk_text: (Secondly) — If it is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death... (Thirdly) — If it is done with the intention of causing bodily injury... sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or— (Fourthly) — If the person committing the act knows that it is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 300 Clauses 2, 3, 4 murder definition. Knowledge of likelihood of death and imminently dangerous acts.

chunk_text: Culpable homicide is not murder if it is committed without premeditation in a sudden fight in the heat of passion upon a sudden quarrel and without the offender having taken undue advantage or acted in a cruel or unusual manner.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 300 Exception 4 sudden fight and heat of passion. When homicide in a quarrel is not murder.

chunk_text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 304A Death by negligence. Punishment for rash or negligent acts causing death.

chunk_text: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 319 Definition of Hurt. Bodily pain, disease, or infirmity caused to a person.

chunk_text: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as “grievous”:— (First) — Emasculation. (Secondly) — Permanent privation of the sight of either eye. (Thirdly) — Permanent privation of the hearing of either ear, (Fourthly) — Privation of any member or joint. (Fifthly) — Destruction or permanent impairing of the powers of any member or joint. (Sixthly) — Permanent disfiguration of the head or face. (Seventhly) — Fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth. (Eighthly) — Any hurt which endangers life or which causes the sufferer to be during the space of twenty days in severe bodily pain, or unable to follow his ordinary pursuits.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 320 Grievous hurt categories. Emasculation, vision loss, hearing loss, disfigurement, fractures, and life-endangering pain.

section_title: Voluntarily causing grievous hurt by use of acid, etc.
chunk_text: Whoever causes permanent or partial damage or deformity to, or burns or maims or disfigures or disables, any part or parts of the body of a person or causes grievous hurt by throwing acid on or by administering acid to that person... shall be punished with imprisonment... which shall not be less than ten years but which may extend to imprisonment for life, and with fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 326A Acid attack punishment. Grievous hurt by throwing or administering acid. Permanent deformity and life imprisonment.

chunk_text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 339 Definition of Wrongful restraint. Obstruction preventing a person from moving in a direction where they have a right to proceed.

chunk_text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceedings beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said 'wrongfully to confine' that person.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 340 Definition of Wrongful confinement. Prevention of person from moving beyond defined limits.

chunk_text: (b) A places men with firearms at the outlets of a building, and tells Z that they will fire at Z if Z attempts leave the building. A wrongfully confines Z.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 340 Illustration confinement by threat of firearms. Example of wrongful confinement.

chunk_text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 341 Punishment for wrongful restraint. Sentence for obstructing a person from proceeding in a direction they have a right to go.

chunk_text: A person is said to use force to another if he causes motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to that other, or if he causes to any substance such motion, or change of motion, or cessation of motion as brings that substance into contact with any part of that other’s body, or with anything which that other is wearing or carrying, or with anything so situated that such contact affects that other’s sense of feeling...
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 349 Definition of Force. Causing motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to another person's body or property.

chunk_text: Whoever intentionally uses force to any person, without that person’s consent, in order to the committing of any offence, or intending by the use of such force to cause, or knowing it to be likely that by the use of such force he will cause injury, fear or annoyance to the person to whom the force is used, is said to use criminal force to that other.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 350 Definition of Criminal force. Using force without consent to commit an offence or cause injury, fear, or annoyance.

chunk_text: Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 351 Definition of Assault. Gesture or preparation causing apprehension of the use of criminal force.

section_title: Assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage her modesty
chunk_text: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than one year but which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 354 Outraging the modesty of a woman. Punishment for assault or criminal force with intent to outrage modesty.

chunk_text: Kidnapping is of two kinds: kidnapping from India, and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 359 Types of Kidnapping: from India and from lawful guardianship.

chunk_text: Whoever takes or entices any minor under sixteen years of age if a male, or under eighteen years of age if a female, or any person of unsound mind, out of the keeping of the lawful guardian of such minor or person of unsound mind, without the consent of such guardian, is said to kidnap such minor or person from lawful guardianship.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 361 Kidnapping from lawful guardianship definition. Taking minor or person of unsound mind without guardian's consent.

chunk_text: Whoever by force compels, or by any deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place, is said to abduct that person.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 362 Definition of Abduction. Compelling by force or inducing by deceit to move from any place.

chunk_text: A man is said to commit 'rape' if he— (a) penetrates his penis, to any extent, into the vagina, mouth, urethra or anus of a woman or makes her to do so with him or any other person; or (b) inserts, to any extent, any object or a part of the body, not being the penis, into the vagina, the urethra or anus of a woman or makes her to do so with him or any other person; or (c) manipulates any part of the body of a woman so as to cause penetration into the vagina, urethra, anus or any part of body of such woman or makes her to do so with him or any other person; or (d) applies his mouth to the vagina, anus, urethra of a woman or makes her to do so with him or any other person, under the circumstances falling under any of the seven descriptions...
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 375 Definition of Rape. Acts of penetration, insertion, or manipulation against a woman's will or without consent.

chunk_text: Sexual intercourse or sexual acts by a man with his own wife, the wife not being under fifteen years of age, is not rape.

references.target: Independent Thought v. Union of India

embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 375 Exception 2 Marital Rape exception. Sexual acts with wife over 15 (now interpreted as 18 via Supreme Court) is not rape.

chunk_text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 378 Theft definition. Dishonest intention to take movable property from possession without consent. [cite: 1075]

chunk_text: Explanation 1.— A thing so long as it is attached to the earth, not being movable property, is not the subject of theft; but it becomes capable of being the subject of theft as soon as it is severed from the earth.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 378 Explanation 1. Property attached to earth becomes subject of theft upon severance. [cite: 1076, 1077]

chunk_text: Explanation 5.— The consent mentioned in the definition may be express or implied, and may be given either by the person in possession, or by any person having for that purpose authority either express or implied.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 378 Explanation 5. Nature of consent in theft: express or implied. [cite: 1081]

chunk_text: (a) A cuts down a tree on Z’s ground, with the intention of dishonestly taking the tree out of Z’s possession without Z’s consent. Here, as soon as A has severed the tree in order to such taking, he has committed theft.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 378 Illustration (a). Severing a tree with dishonest intent constitutes theft. [cite: 1082, 1083]

chunk_text: Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 379 Punishment for theft. Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine. [cite: 1115]

chunk_text: Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property or valuable security, or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits “extortion”.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 383 Extortion definition. Inducing delivery of property through fear of injury. [cite: 1121]

chunk_text: (a) A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 383 Illustration (a). Threatening defamatory libel to obtain money is extortion. [cite: 1122, 1123]

chunk_text: Theft is “robbery” if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 390 When theft is robbery. Use of violence or fear of instant death/hurt during theft. [cite: 1136]

chunk_text: When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery, or where the whole number of persons conjointly committing or attempting to commit a robbery, and persons present and aiding such commission or attempt, amount to five or more, every person so committing, attempting or aiding, is said to commit “dacoity”.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 391 Dacoity definition. Joint commission of robbery by five or more persons.

chunk_text: Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 392 Punishment for robbery. Ten years rigorous imprisonment or fourteen years for highway robbery at night.

section_title: Voluntarily causing hurt in committing robbery
chunk_text: If any person, in committing or in attempting to commit robbery, voluntarily causes hurt, such person, and any other person jointly concerned in committing or attempting to commit such robbery, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 394 Causing hurt while committing robbery. Joint liability and life imprisonment.

chunk_text: If any one of five or more persons, who are conjointly committing dacoity, commits murder in so committing dacoity, every one of those persons shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 396 Dacoity with murder. Death penalty or life imprisonment for all members of the dacoity group.

section_title: Robbery, or dacoity, with attempt to cause death or grievous hurt
chunk_text: If, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses any deadly weapon, or causes grievous hurt to any person, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 397 Minimum seven years for robbery or dacoity with deadly weapon or grievous hurt.

chunk_text: Whoever dishonestly mis-appropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 403 Dishonest misappropriation definition. Converting movable property to own use.

chunk_text: Explanation 2.— A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting if for, or of restoring it to, the owner does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly... but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner...
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 403 Explanation 2. Misappropriation of found property.

chunk_text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits “criminal breach of trust”.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 405 Criminal breach of trust definition. Entrustment of property and dishonest misappropriation in violation of law or contract.

chunk_text: (b) A is a warehouse-keeper. Z going on a journey, entrusts his furniture to A, under a contract that it shall be returned on payment of a stipulated sum for warehouse room. A dishonestly sells the goods. A has committed criminal breach of trust.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 405 Illustration (b) warehouse keeper selling entrusted goods. Example of criminal breach of trust.

chunk_text: Property, the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, or by extortion, or by robbery, and property which has been criminally misappropriated or in respect of which criminal breach of trust has been committed, is designated as “stolen property”, whether the transfer has been made, or the misappropriation or breach of trust has been committed, within or without India.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 410 Definition of Stolen property. Property obtained through theft, extortion, robbery, misappropriation, or breach of trust.

chunk_text: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 411 Punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen property. Knowledge or reason to believe property is stolen.

chunk_text: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to “cheat”.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 415 Definition of Cheating. Deception and dishonest inducement to deliver property or cause harm.

chunk_text: (a) A, by falsely pretending to be in the Civil Service, intentionally deceives Z, and thus dishonestly induces Z to let him have on credit goods for which he does not mean to pay. A cheats.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 415 Illustration (a) pretending to be in Civil Service to obtain credit. Example of cheating.

section_title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
chunk_text: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 420 Punishment for cheating and inducing delivery of property or valuable security. Seven years imprisonment and fine.

chunk_text: Whoever with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits “mischief”. [cite: 1247]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 425 Mischief definition. Intentional destruction or damage to property causing wrongful loss or diminished utility.

chunk_text: Explanation 1.— It is not essential to the offence of mischief that the offender should intend to cause loss or damage to the owner of the property injured or destroyed. It is sufficient if he intends to cause, or knows that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to any person by injuring any property, whether it belongs to that person or not. [cite: 1248, 1249]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 425 Explanation 1. Intent to cause loss to any person (not just owner) constitutes mischief.

chunk_text: Whoever commits mischief shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both. [cite: 1259]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 426 Punishment for mischief. Sentence of up to three months or fine.

chunk_text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit “criminal trespass”. [cite: 1274]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 441 Criminal trespass definition. Unauthorized entry or remaining with intent to commit offense, intimidate, or annoy.

chunk_text: Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit “house-trespass”. [cite: 1275]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 442 House trespass definition. Criminal trespass into building, tent, or vessel used for dwelling or worship.

chunk_text: A person is said to commit “house-breaking” who commits house-trespass if he effects his entrance into the house or any part of it in any of the six ways hereinafter described; or if, being in the house or any part of it for the purpose of committing an offence, or, having committed an offence therein, he quits the house or any part of it in any of such six ways... [cite: 1279, 1280]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 445 House breaking definition. Specific ways of entrance/exit in house trespass for committing an offense.

chunk_text: Whoever makes any false documents or false electronic record or part of a document or electronic record, with intent to cause damage or injury, to the public or to any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause any person to part with property, or to enter into any express or implied contract, or with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed, commits forgery. [cite: 1317]
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 463 Forgery definition. Making false documents or electronic records with fraudulent intent or to cause injury.

chunk_text: First — Who dishonestly or fradulently— (a) makes, signs, seals or executes a document or part of a document; (b) makes or transmits any electronic record or part of any electronic record; (c) affixes any electronic signature on any electronic record; (d) makes any mark denoting the execution of a document or the authenticity of the electronic signature, with the intention of causing it to be believed that such document or part of document, electronic record or electronic signature was made, signed, sealed, executed, transmitted or affixed by or by the authority of a person by whom or by whose authority he knows that it was not made, signed, sealed, executed or affixed;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 464 First making a false document definition. Dishonest or fraudulent making, signing, or sealing of documents or electronic records to simulate authority.

chunk_text: Secondly — Who, without lawful authority, dishonestly or fraudulently, by cancellation or otherwise, alters a document or an electronic record in any material part thereof, after it has been made, executed or affixed with electronic signature either by himself or by any other person, whether such person be living or dead at the time of such alteration;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 464 Secondly making a false document definition. Dishonest or fraudulent material alteration of a document or electronic record without authority.

chunk_text: (a) A has a letter of credit upon B for rupees 10,000 written by Z. A, in order to defraud B, adds a cipher to the 10,000, and makes the sum 1,00,000 intending that it may be believed by B that Z so wrote the letter. A has committed forgery.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 464 Illustration (a) altering letter of credit amount to defraud. Example of making a false document and forgery.

chunk_text: Whoever commits forgery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 465 Punishment for forgery. Sentence of up to two years imprisonment or fine.

section_title: Forgery of valuable security, will, etc.
chunk_text: Whoever forges a document which purports to be a valuable security or a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or which purports to give authority to any person to make or transfer any valuable security, or to receive the principal, interest or dividends thereon, or to receive or deliver any money, movable property, or valuable security, or any document purporting to be an acquittance or receipt acknowledging the payment of money, or an acquittance or receipt for the delivery of any movable property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 467 Forgery of valuable security or will. Aggravated forgery involving financial documents or testamentary instruments with life imprisonment penalty.

section_title: Using as genuine a forged document or electronic record
chunk_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document or electronic record which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document or electronic record, shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document or electronic record.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 471 Using forged document as genuine. Liability for knowingly presenting false documents or records as true.

chunk_text: Whoever, being a clerk, officer or servant, or employed or acting in the capacity of a clerk, officer or servant, wilfully, and with intent to defraud, destroys, alters, mutilates or falsifies any book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security or account which belongs to or is in the possession of his employer, or has been received by him for or on behalf of his employer, or wilfully, and with intent to defraud, makes or abets the making of any false entry in, or omits or alters or abets the omission or alteration of any material particular from or in, any such book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security or account, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 477A Falsification of accounts by employee. Intentional alteration or destruction of employer's financial records or valuable securities.

section_title: Marrying again during lifetime of husband or wife
chunk_text: Whoever, having a husband or wife living, marries in any case in which such marriage is void by reason of its taking place during the life of such husband or wife, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 494 Bigamy definition and punishment. Marrying again during lifetime of spouse when first marriage is valid.

section_title: Husband or relative of husband of a woman subjecting her to cruelty
chunk_text: Whoever, being the husband or the relative of the husband of a woman, subjects such woman to cruelty shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 498A Cruelty by husband or relatives. Punishment for domestic violence and harassment for dowry.

section_title: Husband or relative of husband of a woman subjecting her to cruelty
chunk_text: Explanation.—For the purposes of this section, “cruelty” means— (a) any wilful conduct which is of such a nature as is likely to drive the woman to commit suicide or to cause grave injury or danger to life, limb or health (whether mental or physical) of the woman;
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 498A Explanation (a) Definition of cruelty. Conduct likely to drive woman to suicide or cause grave injury.

chunk_text: Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation of such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 499 Defamation definition. Harm to reputation via spoken or written words, signs, or visible representations.

chunk_text: Explanation 1.—It may amount to defamation to impute anything to a deceased person, if the imputation would harm the reputation of that person if living, and is intended to be hurtful to the feelings of his family or other near relatives.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 499 Explanation 1 Defamation of a deceased person. Imputations hurtful to the feelings of surviving relatives.

chunk_text: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, commits criminal intimidation.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 503 Criminal intimidation definition. Threatening injury to person, reputation, or property to cause alarm or force illegal acts.

section_title: Punishment for attempting to commit offences punishable with imprisonment for life or other imprisonment
chunk_text: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or, as the case may be, one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 511 Punishment for attempts to commit offences. One-half of the maximum punishment for the attempted offence.

section_title: Punishment for attempting to commit offences punishable with imprisonment for life or other imprisonment
chunk_text: (a) A makes an attempt to steal some jewels by breaking open a box, and finds after so opening the box, that there is no jewel in it. He has done an act towards the commission of theft, and therefore is guilty under this section.
embedding_ready_text: IPC Section 511 Illustration (a) attempt to steal from empty box. Criminal attempt towards theft.
